stem: A continuous random variable \( X \) has probability density function \( f(x) = \dfrac{[[p1]]\,x^{[[p]]}}{[[b]]^{[[p1]]}} \) for \( 0 \le x \le [[b]] \) (and \( f(x) = 0 \) otherwise). What is \( E[X] \)?
distractor_rationales: This is the un-normalized integral \( \int_0^b x\cdot x^{p}\,dx \); it forgets the density's normalizing constant entirely.
This uses the exponent \( p \) instead of \( p+1 \) in the numerator's coefficient.
This treats \( X \) as though it were the constant \( b \), ignoring the density shape.
This inverts the fraction \( \frac{p+1}{p+2} \).
This is the mean of a UNIFORM density on \( [0,b] \), ignoring that this density is not flat.
This squares the denominator \( p+2 \) by mistake.

stem: A continuous random variable \( X \) has probability density function \( f(x) = \dfrac{2([[b]]-x)}{[[b]]^{2}} \) for \( 0 \le x \le [[b]] \) (and \( f(x) = 0 \) otherwise), so outcomes near \( 0 \) are more likely than outcomes near \( [[b]] \). What is \( E[X] \)?
distractor_rationales: This is the mean of a UNIFORM density on \( [0,b] \), ignoring that low values are weighted more heavily here.
This is the mean of the mirror-image INCREASING linear density, which weights high values more heavily instead.
This is off by a factor of 2 from the correct mean.
This divides by 4 instead of correctly carrying out the integral.
This treats \( X \) as though it always equals its maximum value \( b \).

stem: A continuous random variable \( X \) is distributed uniformly on the interval \( [[[a]], [[c]]] \). What is \( E[X^2] \), the second moment of \( X \)?
distractor_rationales: This computes \( (E[X])^2 \) instead of \( E[X^2] \); these differ by the variance.
This is the VARIANCE formula \( \frac{(b-a)^2}{12} \), not the second moment.
This drops the cross term \( ac \) and uses the wrong denominator.
This is just \( E[X] \), the first moment, forgetting to square.
This drops the cross term \( ac \) from the correct second-moment formula.

stem: A continuous random variable \( X \) has probability density function \( f(x) = \dfrac{6(x-[[a]])([[b]]-x)}{([[b]]-[[a]])^{3}} \) for \( [[a]] \le x \le [[b]] \) (and \( f(x) = 0 \) otherwise); this density is symmetric about the midpoint \( \mu = [[mu]] \) of the interval. What is \( \operatorname{Var}(X) \)?
distractor_rationales: This is the UNIFORM density's variance formula \( \frac{(b-a)^2}{12} \), misapplied to this non-uniform density.
This divides by 24 instead of the correct denominator for this density's variance.
This divides by 16 instead of the correct denominator for this density's variance.
This is just half the width of the interval, not a variance at all.
This is off by one from the correct variance.
This is off by one from the correct variance.

stem: A continuous random variable \( X \) has probability density function \( f(x) = \dfrac{[[p1]]\,x^{[[p]]}}{[[b]]^{[[p1]]}} \) for \( 0 \le x \le [[b]] \) (and \( f(x) = 0 \) otherwise). Let \( Y = [[c]]X + ([[d]]) \). What is \( E[Y] \)?
distractor_rationales: This uses the UNIFORM mean \( b/2 \) instead of this density's actual mean.
This computes \( c\,E[X] \) but forgets to add the shift \( d \).
This ignores the \( cX \) term entirely, keeping only the constant shift.
This inverts the fraction \( \frac{p+1}{p+2} \) inside \( E[X] \).
This incorrectly multiplies the shift \( d \) by \( c \) as well.
This drops the density's normalizing fraction entirely, treating \( E[X] \) as just \( b \).
